result:
I keep thinking about the moment the light changes.

Not sunrise—everyone writes about sunrise. I mean the smaller, subtler shift that happens at 3:17 on a Tuesday afternoon in October, when the angle of the sun drops another fraction of a degree and suddenly the kitchen wall you’ve stared at for years reveals a crack you swear wasn’t there yesterday. A hairline fracture, fine as a capillary, running from the outlet up to the corner where the paint has always been the color of cooled butter. You stand at the sink holding a wet plate and feel time pour through that crack like cold air through a broken seal. You don’t mention it to anyone because what is there to say? “I think my house is aging faster than I am.” They’d laugh or worry, neither of which helps.

Instead you dry the plate and stack it with the others, and the small aluminum sound it makes settling onto the pile becomes the metronome for whatever life you have left. You count twelve of those sounds—twelve plates, twelve years here, maybe twelve more somewhere else, maybe not. The refrigerator hums in B-flat. The dog clicks across the floor in 3/4 time. You realize you’ve been conducting an orchestra of minor domestic notes, a private symphony no one buys tickets for, and the crack in the wall is the newest percussion, a faint pizzicato that will grow into a drum if you let it.

You don’t let it. You get in the car.

The road is freshly paved, black as printer ink, and the yellow stripes smell like childhood—specifically the smell of the school hallway the day they repainted the gym, and you were seven, and the teacher said the fumes weren’t dangerous but opened the windows anyway, and you watched a monarch butterfly batter itself against the screen until it finally dropped onto the sill, wings opening and closing like a heart that isn’t sure it’s ready to stop. You remember thinking, even then, that the butterfly was extremely small against the vastness of the parking lot beyond the window, and that you were even smaller, and that someday you would be smaller still. The memory arrives intact, uninvited, riding shotgun.

You drive past the new subdivision where they named the streets after things that no longer exist: Fox Hollow Lane, Meadow Crest Court. You imagine the marketing meeting—lattes and anxiety—where a room of people agreed that nostalgia sells better than truth. No one objected. No one ever objects; they just cash the check and go home to their own cracks in their own walls. You wonder what they hear at night. You wonder if any of them have ever stood in a half-painted kitchen and felt the year tilt on its axis at 3:17 p.m.

The grocery store parking lot is a spreadsheet of intentions. Everyone steering carts like dysfunctional proton beams, missing collision by microns. Inside, the fluorescent lights bleach the world into a crime-scene photo. You buy lemons because the color is honest. You buy bread because it’s warm and the bag sighs when you twist it closed. You consider ice cream, then remember the freezer already holds a pint fossilizing under a lid you’ve been meaning to open for three months. You pay, decline a receipt, and leave with the certainty that the cashier has already forgotten the shape of your face.

Back home the crack is still there, but the light has moved on and now it looks like a map of a river you once canoed with someone who spoke only in questions. “What would you save if the water kept rising?” “Which of us will be the first to pretend we weren’t scared?” You capsized that day, laughing, and the river tasted like iron and pine needles. You still don’t know the answers. The crack doesn’t ask; it merely records the fact of separation, a modest historian.

Night arrives without signature. You cook pasta and watch the steam braid itself into ghostly ropes that dissolve against the range hood. You eat standing up, counting fork rotations out of habit: twenty-five per minute, a rhythm instilled by a grandmother who believed that slow chewing extended lifespan. She died at ninety-two, mouth full of pancake, so maybe she was onto something. You rinse the bowl, feed the dog, turn off every light except the one above the stove, the small bulb that stays on like a lighthouse for lost snacks. Its glow reaches the crack and stops, as though respectful of boundaries.

You think about writing a letter to the future occupant of this house. Dear Stranger, there is a fissure in the kitchen wall that appeared one ordinary Tuesday and contains my entire life: every sunrise I ignored, every kiss I meant to return, every ambition I folded smaller than a gum wrapper and slipped into the gap behind the bookcase. If you patch it, please save those scraps. They belong to something larger than drywall. But you don’t write, because words feel heavier than silence and you’ve already carried enough today.

Instead you sit on the back step where the air smells of wet leaves and charcoal. The neighbor’s toddler is crying the way small planets must cry when they realize they’re not the center of anything. The sound is pure, unprocessed grief—no narrative, just voltage. You envy the clarity. Somewhere inside your own chest an identical sound is occurring at a frequency dogs and ex-lovers can hear, but no one mentions it. The stars, polite as ever, keep their distance.

You stay until the grass absorbs the last warmth of the day, until the toddler exhausts itself and the cicadas file their reports. You think: if I died tonight, how long before someone noticed the milk had expired? A week? Two? The imagined sourness makes you laugh, and the laugh surprises you, a small bright fish jumping in the dark. You decide to live at least until the lemons are gone. That feels manageable—four, maybe five days. You can do anything for four days, even inhabit the envelope of your own skin without flinching.

Inside again, you pause at the kitchen doorway. The crack is barely visible now, just a darker line in the dim. You raise your hand anyway, press your palm against it the way you’d steady a frightened horse. The wall is cool, indifferent, but you hold the pose until heat begins to pass between you, a modest negotiation. You whisper, “Not yet.” The house answers with its usual orchestra: the fridge, the dog’s tags, the distant drip in the bathroom sink. You realize you are included in that percussion, your heart ticking reliably, absurdly, on.

You go to bed. You dream of the butterfly, but this time it’s enormous, wingtips brushing both horizons, and when it finally rests the whole sky folds like a letter sliding into an envelope. You wake just before the seal closes, sweating, grateful. Dawn is still hours away. You lie still and listen for the wall, for the faint crack that has begun, quietly, to sing.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
